objects: Built 1868-1870, Marston Cottage was the third of the three “Professors’ Houses”. Its first occupant was Professor William A. Anthony, who resigned in 1872; the second was Alexander Thomson, who resigned in 1884, and the house was shared at times with Albert E. Foote and E.R. Hutchins. General James Rush lived there from 1885 to 1891, after which Anson Marston occupied the house from 1892 until he died in 1949, occasionally sharing it with Samuel W. Beyer. Mrs. Marston lived there until the house was razed in 1958 to make room for the addition to Helser Hall. The brick house included a basement, two full floors, and a large attic. Over time, several modifications were made, including the addition of back stairs in 1874, major repairs in 1904, a screened porch in 1913, and a basement garage in 1923.

Built 1868-1869, South Hall was the first of the three “Professors’ Houses”, and stood on what is now Union Drive. The two-story structure featured a mansard roof, a west-facing front porch, second-story windows that extended into the roof, and a tower-like element at the southwest corner. It was occupied by President Welch from 1869 to 1879, during which time an addition was completed in 1871. After the Welch family moved to The Gables, the building was repurposed for the Botany and Veterinary Medicine departments. In 1880, it was modified for use by Domestic Economy and sustained damage during a tornado in 1882. By 1896, South Hall had become Music Hall, serving that role until it was destroyed by fire in 1912. It was connected to the campus sewer system in 1897.

Built 1868-1870, Music Hall was the second of the three “Professors’ Houses”, and was first occupied by mathematics Professor George W. Jones. Edgar W. Stanton lived with Jones's family, and from 1879 to Stanton's death in 1920, the home was known as the Stanton House. The house was remodeled in 1899 with a 20-foot east addition, repaired in 1904, and updated in 1914 with central heating and a basement garage. After her husband's death, Mrs. Stanton continued to live in the house, and when she started a tea room in 1922, the home became known as "The Maples." Mrs. Stanton moved out in 1924, and the Home Economics Department continued operating the tea room. In 1926, the east (front) porch was enclosed, and the kitchen was enlarged. In 1928, the tearoom closed with the opening of the Memorial Union, and the Music Department moved in. The name "Music Hall" became official in 1929. The house was torn down in 1978 to make way for the new music building, which was constructed on the site.

Built in 1877 where Botany Hall now stands, Professor J.L. Budd built Mortensen Cottage for his family and sold the house to the College for $2,000 in 1884. During this time, a two-story addition was added to this house after a cyclone blew down the chimneys in 1882. Mortensen Cottage was moved in 1892 to make room for the Botany Hall. The house was occupied by several tenants, including J.L. Budd (1877–1890), D.A. Kent (1891–1894), J.B. Weems (1895–1903), G.L. McKay (1904–1907), and Martin Mortensen (1908–1926). It was demolished in 1927 to make way for the new Dairy Industries Building.

Proposed in 1868 due to a lack of proper laboratory space, the Chemical Laboratory was completed in 1871 as a small brick building for approximately $5,000. Between 1874 and 1875, a larger Physical Laboratory was added to the east end. The expanded building housed the Chemistry, Physics, Mechanical, and later Electrical Engineering departments, featuring lecture rooms, laboratories, and an observatory. Additions and upgrades continued, including a second flood in 1891, steam heat in 1892, and a third-floor expansion in 1910 as the building became overcrowded. The laboratory was destroyed by fire on March 25, 1913.

Built between 1864 and 1868, the Main Building was Iowa State’s first major campus structure. Designed by C.A. Dunham after earlier plans were revised, the large brick building housed classrooms, laboratories, a library, a museum, dining facilities, and student and faculty living quarters. An addition completed between 1871 and 1873 expanded its capacity, though the building quickly became overcrowded. Throughout its use, Old Main underwent numerous repairs and upgrades, including heating system replacements, structural reinforcements, and the addition of electric lighting in 1884. Despite these efforts, persistent structural issues and aging infrastructure remained. A fire in December 1900 destroyed the north wing, and a second fire in August 1902 destroyed the south wing, leading to the building’s complete loss. The structure served the college for just over thirty years before being replaced by Beardshear Hall.

Construction of the Cattle Barn started in 1924, but due to a lack of funds, it was completed in late 1926. The barn included silos at the northeast and northwest corners. On October 6, 1931, lightning struck the building, igniting a fire that completely destroyed the barn. The rebuild was completed the following February, and the silo that received the lightning blow was not rebuilt until 1935. Another fire on October 19, 1958, burned the east wing roof and a large quantity of hay. The silos were razed in 1968.

Construction of the Farm House (Knapp-Wilson House) began in 1860 and was completed in 1865 as the first building on Iowa State’s campus. Built by day labor, it served as an early residence for farm and college operations. The structure was expanded and modified over time, including additions, repairs, and conversion into a two-family residence in the late 1880s. After decades of use and continued updates, it was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1965. Restored between 1972 and 1975, the building now serves as a museum preserving Iowa State’s early history.

As enrollment increased and the Main Building could no longer accommodate all students, proposals were made for a dormitory to house 120 students. Ten years later, funds were made available, and Boarding Cottage West was erected in 1880, consisting of 16 rooms. An addition was completed in 1882, the porch was added in August 1886, and in 1894, both floors of the cottage were remodeled. A year later, three rooms in the basement were made available for an electrical laboratory. The cottage was remodeled again in 1907 to serve as the College Hospital, until the new Hospital was built in 1918, when Boarding Cottage West became the detention ward. Further remodeling took place in 1920, and the building was razed in 1934.

The Feeding Barn was built in 1880. Designed by Prof T.L. Smith, the Feeding Barn was constructed under contract with F.S. Whiting at a total cost of $792.10. The barn was authorized for repairs in 1890, funded by Experiment Station resources. In October 1901, a fire originating in the barn destroyed the structure along with the Experiment Station Barn.

In 1881, Professor Budd requested to purchase horses and build a shelter for them, though only the horses were initially approved. Budd built the barn himself and was reimbursed by the Board in 1882. The barn was practically destroyed by a cyclone on April 8, 1882, and was moved to the gardener’s house in 1897 with the recommendation that the barn be repaired.

Built in 1878 as a one-acre farm workman’s cottage, Eastwood Cottage was originally leased to L.L. Eastwood. He later offered to sell the cottage, and it was purchased by the college in 1882. The house was being used by the Experiment Station in 1888, and was razed sometime between 1888 and 1896, as it no longer appears on later campus maps.

This house was built in 1877 by Chemistry Professor Thomas E. Pope, on the south side of what is now Lincoln Way. Pope sold the property to the College in 1884. Professor Wynn had been renting Pope Cottage before the sale, and remained there through 1885. The frame house consisted of two stories and a basement. Plumbing and electricity were installed in 1902, and the porch was added in 1924.

Built in 1880, North Hall was constructed to house the Agriculture, Veterinary Science, and Botanical departments. Completed for occupancy in 1881, the building was heavily damaged by a tornado in 1882 but was quickly repaired. A porch was added in 1886, and two years later, a water supply and coal stoves were installed. After Margaret Hall was built in 1894, North Hall became an attached wing of the new building, with spaces converted for kitchen use, domestic economy functions, and servants’ rooms. North Hall was razed in 1926.

Built in 1879-1880 as a home for President Welch and his family, The Gables was later purchased by Professor Millikan Stalker and eventually passed to Iowa State College in 1928. Renamed International House, it served as a residence for international male students. The building sustained significant fire damage in 1951 but was repaired and continued in use until it was intentionally demolished in 1963 to make way for Buchanan Hall.

Built in 1892, The Hub originally served as a depot, bookstore, and post office to support the Ames and College Railway. After the railway was discontinued in 1908, the building was relocated and continued to function as a bookstore and post office, with multiple additions made in 1920, 1946, and 1952. The bookstore moved to the Memorial Union in 1958, and the building remained a post office until 1963. Renamed “The Hub” in 1959, it was repurposed for food services and later housed additional campus functions, including a copy center, ticket office, and traffic office.

Built in 1892, Botany Hall comprised four stories and a basement used for horticulture. The first floor housed a livestock room and the agricultural chemistry and horticulture departments, while the second floor was used for offices and recitation rooms. The third floor housed veterinary offices, laboratories, and a museum, while the top floor was used by literary and scientific societies. By 1897, Botany Hall showed signs of structural issues, including floor settlement and plaster cracking. A Farm Mechanics and Soil Physics addition was built in 1903. The building was mainly used for storage after the Agricultural Engineering department moved in in 1922. The building was renamed Botany Hall, and the Botany department used it from 1928 to 1967, followed by the Seed Laboratory until 1977, and the Psychology department in 1968.

The second Creamery was built between 1891 and 1893 to replace the original creamery. Initial funding was insufficient, requiring additional appropriations in 1892 to complete workspaces, laboratories, and dormitory rooms above the facility. Minor changes and repairs were made, and an addition was constructed in 1898. By 1903, the building was considered outdated and too costly to maintain. It was authorized for demolition in 1904, remained in use briefly, and was razed in 1905.

Teamster's Cottage #2 was built in 1890, where Bessey Hall now stands. It was initially constructed as housing for Experiment Station employees, and was occupied by Capt. Speer and his family. In 1900, interior modifications were recommended, and in 1915, the house was relocated to a site across from the power plant, where it became one of a group of five cottages. Teamster's Cottage #2 was razed in 1970.

Morrill Hall was built from 1890 to 1890 to fill the need for a library, museum, and chapel. The building was named in honor of the Senator who had sponsored the Morrill bill, which established the land-grant college system. The hall was used by the zoology, entomology, and geology departments, and included a gymnasium, chapel, library, and a barber shop from 1905 to 1908. Alterations were made in 1914 when the library was removed and replaced by Agricultural Extension offices. Following safety concerns and renovations in 1922, more alterations were made to Morrill Hall in 1938 and 1955.

Bevier House was constructed in 1888 and included two stories and an attached propagating house. The house was assigned to the music department from December 1892 to May 1894, when the Botany department moved in, and the building was remodeled. In 1896, Bevier House moved, became a home for Margaret Hall's employees, and was known as the Klatter Klub. The house moved again in 1910 and was occupied by Thomas Sloss until 1924. Bevier House was moved again between December 1924 and March 1925, becoming the Isabel Bevier Home Management House, only to be moved again. In its final location, Bevier House served multiple purposes, including a home management house (1953 and 1955–1956), housing for graduate women (1953–1955 and 1956–1961), and office space for retired faculty members.

Coburn House was constructed in 1888 on a one-acre leased site. and occupied by the Knapp family. In 1896, a water line to the house was authorized, and an addition was built in 1900. The house was sold to the college in 1920 and remodeled for use as a Home Economics Division practice cottage. The building was moved in 1925 and named the Mary B. Welch Home Management House, before being renamed the Gertrude Coburn Home Management House in 1928. The house was moved again in 1947 and continued to serve the Home Management function until the 1950s, when it was used for genetics research and the Psychology department. Coburn House was condemned in the early 1970s and razed in 1975.

The Greenhouse was built as an attached unit when Bevier House was constructed in 1888. When Bevier House was moved in 1896, the propagating house remained in place. In the spring of 1897, plans to move the structure were considered, but they were ultimately abandoned, and the greenhouse was demolished later that year. Materials from the structure were reused or sold, and the site was graded.

The Veterinary Hospital was constructed between 1884 and 1885 as a two-story building with additional space designed for animal care. Located approximately where the Pine Room is currently situated in the Memorial Union, the building initially served as a veterinary hospital, including clinics, animal stalls, and surgical spaces. As enrollment increased, the building became inadequate and underwent several updates, including connection to the central water supply in 1898. The Veterinary Department relocated in 1912, and the building was repurposed for storage. Between 1912 and 1921, a one-story shed was constructed for additional storage, and the Veterinary Hospital became known as the Carpenter Shop. The building was razed in 1926 to make room for the new Memorial Union Building.

Built between 1884 and 1885, the Sanitary Building served as a campus hospital, with additional use by the Veterinary Department until 1893. After the Veterinary Department relocated, the building was used exclusively as a hospital and underwent major remodeling in 1900. Following a 1902 fire, repairs were completed, and the Sanitary Building continued to operate as a hospital until 1907, when it was repurposed as a rooming house. The building, known as Music Hall, served the Music Department from 1912 until it was razed in 1927.

Built in 1884, the English Office Building was originally designed to house the offices of the President, Secretary, and Treasurer. An addition was constructed in 1892, and the office spaces were rented to staff personnel in the following years. Although various repairs were completed, including the installation of a new furnace in 1904, the Public Grounds Committee recommended removing the English Office Building in 1905, a recommendation that was never carried out. In 1908, the building housed the college book store, the custodian, and the Head of the Military Department. The Office Building was connected to the central heating system in 1914, while it operated as the headquarters for the Buildings and Grounds department, and was rented to YMCA food service employees. The Music Department used the space until 1928, and in 1929, the second floor served as office space for the English Department. In 1973, the English and Speech offices were relocated, and the Office Building became the headquarters for International Education Services.

The original Veterinary Barn was constructed in 1883 by F. Turner. After a new Veterinary Hospital was built in 1885, the original barn was repurposed for use by the boarding department. The Veterinary Barn was transferred to the Agricultural Department in 1892 and relocated. By 1893, the building had been moved again and repaired, though the barn's final location is unknown.

Construction of the Gymnasium began in 1883, and in May of that year, a student committee requested funds from the Board of Regents to complete construction and purchase equipment. The building was completed in August of 1883; however, the Gymnasium Association never raised enough funds to pay off the building's debt. In March 1886, the Gymnasium was moved to Professor Mount’s property in payment for his financing the construction.

Sloss House was constructed between 1882 and 1883, with a porch addition in 1884. Professor Charles E. Bessey was the first occupant of the house and oversaw early renovations, such as sheeting the house with tarred paper and building a woodshed, wellhouse, and a privy. Minor repairs were made in 1897, 1904, and 1915, and extensive renovations, including porch modifications and the addition of a garage, were completed in 1925. Over time, Sloss House served a variety of functions, ranging from faculty residences to student housing and academic use.

Construction of the Laboratory of Mechanics began in 1882 and continued until 1883, when it was found to be too small to accommodate the growing engineering departments. An expansion was finished in 1885, and the basement was remodeled between 1898 and 1899 to create a hydraulics laboratory. Major renovations followed in 1907, with toilet facilities added in 1923 and a one-story addition in 1933. Further remodeling in 1967 included the addition of office space and a classroom, improved laboratories, and fire escapes on the upper floors.

The Grounds Cottage was built in 1882, with a porch added in 1886 and a water line extended to the house in 1892. It was first occupied by gardener and custodian Jerry Sexton, followed by other grounds department employees in the years that followed. The cottage was moved in 1905 to make way for a new horticulture building and relocated again in 1924. It remained in this location until it was razed in 1970.

The Farm Laborer’s Cottage was built in 1882 to serve the farm and horticulture departments. Repairs were made in the 1890s, including new siding, interior work, and painting. The house was occupied by William Pierce, Fred Leighton, and Professor G.L. McKay at various times. The Farm Laborer’s Cottage was sold and removed from campus in 1904.

The East Boarding Cottage was built in 1882 to accommodate additional students, reflecting the success of the Boarding Cottage West. The boarding house filled quickly, providing low-cost housing for students. A porch was added in 1886, and renovations completed in the 1890s expanded and updated the interior. In 1897, the East Boarding Cottage was converted into teacher housing, and in 1901, it became the football team's living quarters. Steam heat was included in 1905.

Built in 1879, the Farm Boarding Club building was constructed for the Horticulture Department. The two-story building included lecture rooms and office spaces, a Horticulture museum, and several storage rooms. The Horticulture Department relocated in 1894, and the Farm Boarding Club was remodeled into a residence. It became a boarding club by 1895, with water and sewer auditions in 1896. It continued as a faculty club until 1906, when it was reserved for potential hospital use. The building was moved in 1915 and later used as an employee boarding house and residence. The Farm Boarding Club building remained in use until it was razed in 1970.

Fair Oaks Mansion was built by students in 1879 as a small residence on the college farm. It was relocated in 1884 or 1885 and became known as the Bus House, where it was used for student housing in the early 1890s. The building was moved again in 1893 and used as a gardener’s residence. By the late 1890s, the Fair Oaks Mansion had fallen into poor condition and was eventually removed by 1899.

The Power Station was built in 1891 as a boiler house, with an addition in 1897 that expanded it to include a pumping station as part of the campus water system. Additional boilers and expansions were added in 1899 and 1902. The building housed the electrical generating plant and pumping station and served as a laboratory for the Mechanical Engineering Department. The Power Station was removed in 1912 to make room for the new Mechanical Engineering Building.

The first Creamery was built in 1879, with an addition in 1882 that included a creamery, ice house, and cold storage building. The building was moved and remodeled in 1891 to serve as the farm foreman’s residence and a boarding house for farm workmen. The first Creamery was razed in 1927 to make way for the Dairy Industries Building.

The first Horse Barn was constructed in 1870 to fulfill the need for a stable. The brick structure included stalls, a loft, a harness room, and a granary. Funds for expansion were requested in 1897. The original Horse Barn was removed in 1900 to make way for a new barn.

The Workshop was built in 1870 at the request of President Welch for a space large enough to accommodate students who take Mechanic Arts courses. The two-story structure included an engine house and two laundry rooms until it was moved in 1885, at which point the laundry rooms were torn down and rebuilt. By 1894, the building was called The Carpenter and Pattern Shop and had experienced several small additions. Concerns for the condition of the Workshop were raised in 1893, but the building was not removed until 1898, after the completion of Building 'A' and the Carpenter Shop.

Built in 1894, the Experimental Barn supported the college’s growing farm and animal husbandry work. The barn had a short life, as a fire on October 25, 1901, burned down the Cattle and Experimental Barns.

Margaret Hall was built between 1894 and 1895 as an undergraduate women’s residence hall, marking a major expansion of campus housing. The building included modern amenities and dining facilities and was fully occupied upon opening. From 1902 until 1906, Margaret Hall also housed the Botany Department after a fire in the Main Building. Several renovations were completed, including fire escapes in 1897 and 1903, lockers and shower baths in 1913, and a swimming pool in 1915. By 1929, the dining services in Margaret Hall were discontinued, and housing was reserved for graduate women. The building was destroyed by a fire on April 9, 1938.

The Greenhouse was built in 1896, with an expansion added in 1903 to increase space for horticulture programs. Additional structures for potting and storage were added as the facility grew. The Greenhouse was removed between 1967 and 1968.

Building ‘A’ was constructed in 1897 as a forge shop and foundry for the Mechanical Engineering Department. The building remained in use as a foundry until 1936, after which it served as the Technical Institution's headquarters. Building ‘A’ was removed in 1973.

Construction of the Campanile began in 1897 and was originally intended to house a gift of ten bells from Professor E.W. Stanton in memory of Margaret McDonald Stanton. The tower was completed in 1898, the clock was added in 1899, and the chimes played for the first time in 1900. In 1929, twenty-six more bells were added, and the structure was named the Edgar W. and Margaret MacDonald Stanton Memorial Carillon of Bells. Thirteen treble bells were installed in 1956, and another in 1967, bringing the total to 50 bells. The Campanile stands 110 feet high.

Osborn Cottage was built from 1882 to 1883 as one of two faculty residences. The house underwent multiple upgrades, including the installation of a furnace and storm windows in 1898, and the addition and repair of a porch in 1914 and 1920. A nearby shed was remodeled into a garage and sleeping porch in 1924. Osborn Cottage housed various professors and campus groups over time and was later used for programs such as the Student Alumni Association, the Landscape Architecture program, and the Honors Program.

Built in 1898 and originally known as the Carpenter Shop, the Aerospace Laboratory provided students with tool-based instruction. It was later known as the Pattern Shop until 1932, and in 1934, its function shifted to the Aeronautical Engineering Laboratory. The building was removed in 1973.

Engineer’s Cottage, often referred to as Edgerton House, was built in 1898 as a residence for J.J. Edgerton. It was updated with plumbing and steam heat in 1904 and 1913, moved in 1915, and housed several faculty members until it was razed in 1970.

Kildee Cottage was built in 1900 and first occupied in 1901 by Dr. O.H. Cessna under a campus lease agreement. The house was purchased by the college in the spring of 1915, and it later became the longtime residence of Herbert H. Kildee. After 1965, Kildee Cottage served as the headquarters for the building services section of the Physical Plant before being removed in 1973.

The Knoll was built from 1900 to 1901 as the official residence for the college president, but it served multiple roles over time, including temporary use by the Music Department. The house underwent numerous renovations and expansions, including the addition of the garage and porch. Although its future use was debated, the Knoll was remodeled in 1966 and continued as the president’s residence.

The Landscape Architecture building was constructed in 1900 as a Horse Barn and Livestock Judging Pavilion to support the expanding agricultural programs. It served its original function for nearly 30 years before being remodeled into the headquarters for Landscape Architecture in 1930. The building remained in use by the Landscape Architecture Department until 1978, and the space was later used for agronomy and journalism overflow.

Marston Hall was built between 1900 and 1903 as the college’s new Engineering Hall. It was designed by Proudfoot & Bird after being selected through a formal design competition. The building was occupied in 1903 and later repaired after damage from a 1909 blizzard. The name Marston Hall was officially adopted in 1947, with additional renovations completed in 1955, 1972, and 1973.

Norton House was built around 1900 by Professor Lizzie May Allis as a private residence. The house changed ownership several times before being purchased by the college in 1921 and used as a practice cottage, when it was known as Gray Cottage. It was renamed the Alice P. Norton Home Management House in 1929, moved twice, and was eventually used as the headquarters for the Film Production Unit.

The Theater-Workshop (also known as the Judging Pavilion, Stock Pavilion, and later Shattuck Theater) was built in 1900 as a stock judging pavilion. In 1931, the building was converted into a theater workshop for the Iowa State Players and remodeled in 1936. The name Shattuck Theater was adopted in 1960, and the structure remained in use until it was razed in 1979.

Hog Houses on campus date back to the 1860s, with the first hog pen built in 1865, and a piggery completed in 1867. Additional structures were added over time, including a swine house in 1880, though early facilities were poorly documented. After the Piggery was destroyed by fire in 1885, a new hog house was built in 1891 and remained in use until it burned in 1922.
Photograph of the Farm Boarding Club

Photograph looking south-east at the Farm Boarding Club when the building was located at the corner of Beach and Wallace. Part of the Teamster's Cottage #2 can be seen to the left (east side) of the Farm Boarding Club. Annotation: "Farm Boarding Cottage, 1957."
Photograph of the Farm Boarding Club when it was Horticulture Hall
Photograph of the Farm Boarding Club when the building was located on Morrill and was known as Horticulture Hall. Annotation: "Faculty Club stood where Library is now. Farm Boarding Club."
Photograph of the front porch of the Farm House

Photograph looking north-west at the front porch and south side of the Farm House. Part of Catt Hall can be seen in the background to the left (north-west) of the Farm House. Annotation: "Farm House."
Photograph of the north-west corner of the Farm House
Photograph of the north-west corner of the Farm House with Dinkey tracks in the foreground. Annotation: "Horse barn redesigned in 1930 for Landscape architecture."
Photograph of a snowy pathway looking east between the Farm House and Landscape Architecture

Photograph of a pathway cleared through the snow running east between the Farm House and Creamery, First (when it was probably known as the Farm Foreman’s Cottage) on the right (south side), and Landscape Architecture (when it was known as the Horse Barn) on the left (north side). Annotation: “Looking east Farm House on right, Horse Barn on left, after 1907 where the electric line turned north of Ag Hall & Margaret Hall.”
Photograph of the front of the Farm House
Photograph of the front of the Farm House showing the lawn in front of the house. Annotation: "Farm House, Horse & Cattle barns in background on right. n.d."
Photograph of the west side of the Farm House

Photograph showing the south-west corner of the Farm House including both the west and south porches. The Creamery, First can be seen in the background to the right (west) of the Farm House. Annotation: "The Farm House, picture appears in 1915 Alumnus."
Photograph of the flagpole being raised

Photograph showing the raising of the flagpole on central campus. In the background Bearshear Hall, Marston Hall, the Marston Water Tower, the Hub, and Mirrill Hall can be seen. Annotation: "Erecting of steel flagpole, ca. March 1908. Beardshear Hall, Marston Hall, water tower, Hub, and Morrill Hall in background."

Photograph showing the raising of the flagpole on central campus. In the background Beardshear Hall, Marston Hall, the Marston Water Tower, and the Hub can be seen. Annotation: “Raising of flagpole. Bearshear Hall, Marston Hall, water tower & Hub, 1908.”

Photograph of people pouring cement for a driveway. The buildings in the background, from left to right (west to north) are Margaret Hall, North Hall, MacKay Hall, and the Bevier House. Annotation: “Flagpole, n.d.”
Photograph of the west side of the Farm House

Photograph showing the south-west corner of the Farm House including both the west and south porches. The Creamery, First can be seen in the background to the right (west) of the Farm House. Annotation: “Farm House ca. 1911.”
Photograph of the south side of the Farm House

Photograph of the south side of the Farm House showing the front porch. The Cattle Barn, First can be seen in the background to the right (north-east) of the Farm House. Annotation: “Farm House. Cattle barns in background. Sept. 22, 1905 from south.”
Photograph of an arbor with a barn behind it in the background. Annotation: “Summer House at Farm House. May 27, 1907.”
Photograph of Dinkey tracks running behind the Farm House
Photograph of Dinkey tracks as they run behind (north of) the Farm House. Annotation: “Farm House 1906. Motor track in foreground.”
Photograph overlooking the rear of the orginial Champlin Store and livery

Photograph of the rear of the original Champlin store and livery located at the corner of Lincoln Way and Welch. In the background Marston Cottage can be seen to the left (north-west) while to the right (north) the Marston Water Tower rises above engineering buildings in the distance. Annotation: “House, barn? on campus. Water tower in background ca. 1912.”
Photograph of three people and three Percheron horses

Photograph of two people holding leads for three Percheron horses while a third person stands nearby. The Cattle Barn, First can be seen in the background with a windmill rising behind it. Annotation: “Percheron horses. Windmill, Cattle Barn (farm barn) ca. 1913.”
Photograph of a person holding a lead for a Percheron horse

Photograph of a person holding a lead for a Percheron horse in front of the Cattle Barn, First. A little bit of a silo can be seen behind the person and a windmill towers above the barn. Annotation: "Prized percheron. Cattle Barn (farm barn) in background ca. 1913."
Photograph of three people leading three Percheron horses

Photograph of three people leading three Percheron horses between the Cattle Barn, First and the Cattle Barn machine shed. Part of a silo can be seen behind the Cattle Barn and a windmill rises above the Cattle Barn. Annotation: "Machine Shed left background. Iowa Silo built around 1909 behind Cattle Barn (farm barn), Percheron horses ca. 1913.”
Photograph of three people holding leads for three Percheron horses
Photograph of three people holding leads for three Percheron horses in front of the Cattle Barn, First. Annotation: “Percheron horses, Cattle Barn (farm barn) in background ca. 1913.”
Photograph of the Dinkey tracks as they run west passed the Theater Workshop and Landscape Architecture

Photograph looking west at Dinkey tracks. To the right (north) the Theater Workshop (Judging Pavilion) and Landscape Architecture (Horse Barn, First) can be seen. The Farm House can be seen in the background to the left (south). Annotation: “Motor track looking west. Horse Barn (Land. Arch. Bldg.), theater pavilion on right. Farmhouse on left in background. n.d.”
Photograph of the Landscape Architecture (Horse Barn)
Photograph of the Landscape Architecture building (Horse Barn), with the Cattle Barn in the background and the Theater Workshop on the right. The view is looking northeast.
Photograph of sheep in winter pasture
Photograph of sheep in a snow-covered pasture, with the cattle feeding barn in the background. The view is looking north-northwest.
Photograph of horses grazing on the south campus area
Photograph of horses grazing on the south campus area, with sheep barns in the left background, and the hog house in the right background. Looking north, with a creek in the mid-ground.
Photograph of the early Sheep Barn
Photograph of the early Sheep Barn showing the exterior of the barn with fenced yards and sheep in the pens. The view is looking northwest.
Photograph of the Cattle Barn looking north-northeast.
Photograph of the Horse Barn and Cattle Barn

Photograph of the south and wet elevations of the Horse Barn (Landscape Architecture) on the right and the Cattle Barn in the background. The view is looking northeast. Leafless trees appear in the foreground.
Photograph of the First Horse Barn
Photograph of the first horse barn from Andreas Atlas. The view is looking southwest.
Etching of the Piggery from 1884 catalog
Etching showing the Piggery from the 1884 catalog depicting the swine breeding barn. Annotation: "Piggery 1884 catalog."

Photograph of cows on a path with the Experiment Station Barn in the far left background and feeding shed in the close left background. A tall silo also appears in the photograph. The view is looking south.
Photograph of cows on a path with the Theater Workshop (Judging Pavilion) in the right background. The view is looking south.
Photograph of the Hog Barn and the Pavilion
Photograph of the Hog Barn and the Pavilion with swine grazing outside.
Photograph of the Hog Pavilion with swine grazing outside.
Photograph of the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn
Photograph of the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn with several horses and people in the foreground, and the first cattle barn in the background.
Photograph of the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn

Photograph of a group of people standing with cattle in front of the Judging Pavilion. The horse barn is in the left background. The view is looking northwest.

Etchings showing the horse, cattle, and swine barns, and the creamery. Images show general views of the buildings, with one showing horses, people, and livestock in the foreground in front of barns. Annotation: "Picture appears in 1883 catalogue. A swine house burned about 1885 and was probably the piggery. Horse Barn built 1970 Cattle Barn built 1861."

Photograph showing the barn complex at the Agricultural Experiment Station. It shows the Judging Pavilion, the Experiment Station Barn, the Cattle Barn, the current Theater Workshop, and the current Landscape Architecture building.
Photograph of the Cattle Barn and the Horse Barns facing north-northeast
Photograph showing the Cattle Barn, the Horse Barn, and farm area, looking north-northeast. A person walks with two horses beside the barn.
Photograph of a west-northwest view of the barn complex. It shows the horse and cattle barns, a pavilion, and an outbuilding, with fenced pastures in the foreground.
Photograph of horses and carriages in from of the Cattle Barn

Photograph of several carriages and teams of horses in front of the Cattle Barn, Annotation: "Cattle barns renovated in 1898. Another photo shows the L.A. Building was built when this picture was taken."
Photograph of Sloss House looking east-southwest in winter.
Photograph of Sloss House, looking east
Photograph of Sloss House in the winter. A person stands beside a tree in the snow.
Photograph of Sloss House with a pile of bricks in the foreground.
Photograph of women outside Sloss House
Photograph of women outside Sloss House while it was being used as a home management house. One woman leans over a baby carriage, while others stand on the steps and sidewalk.

Photograph of the south doors of Shattuck Theatre. The doors are closed, and the sign above the door says "Jane Eyre."

Photograph of the south doors of Shattuck Theatre. The doors are closed, and the sign above the door says "Jane Eyre."
Eight photographs of Shattuck Theatree in the fall, taken from different angles.
Views of the Chemical Building, East Boarding College, and construction of Alumni Hall and Central Building, 1906

Campus buildings viewed from left to right: Chemical and Physical Laboratory; East Boarding Cottage, Alumni Hall, and Central Building (later known as Beardshear Hall). Alumni Hall and Central Building are under construction. There are people walking along the dirt paths, 1906.
Aerial view of group of buildings

View southwest from Main Building. The sidewalk leads to Chemical and Physical Laboratory, built in 1870 & 75, it burned in 1913. East and West Cottages, men's dormitories, are to the left of it. East Cottage, built in 1882, was later used as Training Quarters for athletes. It was torn down in 1907. West Cottage, built in 1880, was later used by the College hospital. It was torn down in 1934. In back of Chemical Hall is the Engineering Building, built 1883 & 1884. Later it was known as the Theoretical and Applied Mechanics Laboratory and now known as Mechanics Laboratory. To the right of Engineering Hall is the frame workshop, built in 1870. It was torn down in 1897. The house in the trees is the Marston Cottage which was torn down after Mrs. Marston's death in 1958 when it was almost surrounded by Friley Hall. Tennis courts and the football field supplanted the orchards.
Construction of the west wing of State Gymnasium viewed from the northwest

A dirt road runs along the front (north) of State Gymnasium. Work is continuing on the doors and windows on the west wing of the building. Chemical and Physical Hall is on the far left before it burnt down in 1913 and Grounds Cottage is on the right. Grounds Cottage was moved across from the Heating Plant in 1924.

Two horse-drawn carriagesare passing in front of Ladies Hall (later known as Margaret Hall). Botany Hall (later known as Carrie Chapman Catt Hall) is on the far right. The Quadrangle is behind Botany Hall, 1900.
The Old Cattle Barn is shown in this photograph dated March 22, 1929.
Horse Barn and Old Cattle Barn
To the left of the Horse Barn is shown a teamster driving a draft team past the Old Cattle Barn.
Horses and foals outside of the Horse Barn. The Cattle Barn is in the background.
Handler posing a gray Percheron stallion (most likely Elmer Koons with Jalap, the chief herd sire for Iowa State College [University] in the 1920's) in the front of the Old Cattle Barn.
General view of barns and yards near Dairy Industries, circa 1884
View of barns and yards near Dairy Industries, circa 1884.

Students are examining plant specimens with microscopes in the Botany Lab located in part of the old chapel of the Main Building (Old Main). Professor Pammel is bending over looking through a microscope in the center of the photograph. There is a map and posters showing the anatomy of plants hanging on the walls.

The front side of Old Main, with the college military cadet corps arranged in a review formation on the lawn in front of the building. An officer on a horse is at the foot of the Old Main steps. Military training was compulsory for male Iowa State students from 1868 through 1962.

The front side of Old Main, with the college military cadet corps arranged in a review formation on the lawn in front of the building. The women's military corps is in front of the corps, in addition to a band and a drum major. Military training was required of Iowa State male students from 1868 through 1962 and women also organized a military corps from 1878 through 1897.

An aerial view of the rear side of Old Main. A flag flies from the north tower of the building. The Hub and railroad tracks are depicted to the left of Old Main. Further to the left of the image can be seen the rear of Morrill Hall.

The north side of Old Main after a fire in 1900. A large number of chairs are strewn around the grounds. This view shows clearly that the north roof and north wall of the buildings are gone. Window openings on the west and south side of the north wing are empty of glass and framing.
The south wall of the south wing of Old Main after the fire in December 1900. Large amounts of building contents are on the grounds. People near the building are looking through the remains.

Old Main from the south after the second fire in August 1902. The gutted building stands behind a large heap of rubble, on which a man is standing. Seven men and two boys are in the foreground, all looking at the building remains.

The south side of the south wing of Old Main after the first fire in December 1900. A large amount of room furnishings are strewn around the grounds. This view shows clearly that the south wing was the least affected portion of the building in the first fire.

Old Main from the southeast after the second fire in August 1902. The gutted building stands behind a large heap of rubble. Only the front porch is still intact. The water tower is seen to the left of the building in the middle distance.
Old Main on fire at 6:30 am on the December day in 1900 that the first fire occurred. The building appears to be completely engulfed in flames, although it is only the North Wing that was damaged.
Old Main during deep winter following the December 1900 fire. The North wing has largely disappeared. The South Wing of the building is still in use. The ground is covered in snow.
Old Main and Morrill Hall (constructed 1890-1891). A number of people are in front of each building as well as walking to the buildings.
Old Main and Morrill Hall, between 1897 and 1900
Looking southwest from Botany Hall toward Old Main and Morrill Hall. Taken between 1897 and 1900.
Old Chemistry building, Maples, President's residence, and Old Main, circa 1876

This picture "taken about '70" probably was taken in 1876. Large building at right rear is the Main Building, built in 1868, with an additional wing built in 1871. It burned about the turn of the century, and was replaced by a new limestone building which was first known as Central and now is known as Beardshear Hall (after an early president). Beardshear is near center of present campus and is headquarters for the administration of the college as well as classroom building. Building in foreground is president's residence, built in 1869. At far left (rear) is the Maples, built in 1869, and now known as Music Hall, the headquarters of the music department. (It is only building visible in picture still standing.) In left center toward rear is Chemistry Building, built in 1870 with an addition in 1875. Smaller building behind Chemistry is not completely identified.
An aerial view of Old Main and Morrill Hall from Central campus.
An aerial view of Old Main (later known as Central Building and Beardshear Hall) in the center and Morrill Hall on the right. Other campus buildings are to the left of Old Main.
Photograph of South Hall, the former home of President Welch and, later, the building where veterinary science was first taught
View of campus looking west prior to 1913 when Chemical Hall burned and before the streets were paved.

In this view looking west, Alumni Hall is on the left, Chemical and Physical Laboratory is to the left of the smoke stack, and the light-colored building on the right is Engineering Hall II (later known as Marston Hall). A man is attending to his horse on the unpaved street in the center of this view and there are rows of homes in the background, 1913.
Views of Chemical Building, East Boarding College, and construction of Alumni Hall and Central Building, 1906

Campus buildings viewed from left to right: Chemical and Physical Laboratory, East Boarding Cottage, Alumni Hall, and Central Building (later known as Beardshear Hall). Alumni Hall and Central Building are under construction. There are people walking along the dirt paths, 1906.
The Water Tower construction site, March 22, 1897, about 4:30 PM. Old Main and the Chemical and Physical Laboratory can be seen in the background.

A quantitative analysis class in the Chemistry-Physics Hall, probably in 1890s. The caption on the back of the photograph states: "Note blast lamp for igniting crucibles; Kipp gas generator, wash bottle made form round bottom flask."
The 1893 Dairying class of 36 students and Professors F.A. Leighton and G.W. McKay standing in front of the creamery building.
The northeast corner of the Knoll is in the center of this image. Ivy covers the tower and a horse and carriage is parked under the porte cochere.

A unit of U.S. soldiers from Missouri, the "Motor Truck Second Section." Twenty seven men and two dogs are posed for a photograph by a military transport vehicle. The truck's canvas cover is stenciled with the insignia "U.S.Q.M.C." The tower of the Laboratory of Mechanics, the Student Services Building, and the West Boarding Cottage can be seen in the background.
The Botany class is sitting in a North Hall classroom. The women students are sitting near the front of the room. Microscopes, books, and some flowers are in vases on the instructor's desk.
High-level view of Margaret Hall, Carrie Chapman Catt Hall, and Morrill Hall
High-level view of Margaret Hall, Carrie Chapman Catt Hall, and Morrill Hall.
Carrie Chapman Catt Hall (Agricultural Hall) in winter, 1918
This winter scene depicts Catt Hall with the surrounding trees in the foreground. MacKay and Margaret Halls can also be seen.
Photograph of North Hall, which housed the Veterinary Division and Botany Department from 1880 - 1885
View of Engineering Hall, Power Station, and State Gymnasium, March 2, 1923

Engineering Hall I (later known as Laboratory of Mechanics) is the second building to the left of the Power Plant. State Gymnasium and Clyde Williams Field are in the background to the right, March 2, 1923.
The Campanile in January 1949. Long shadows play on the snow between the camera and the tower. The clock shows 10:10. Sloss House can be seen to the left of the Campanile, both enshrouded by trees.
Photograph of the Sloss House, home to the Sloss Women's Center since 1981.
Old Main, Morrill Hall, and Margaret Hall
Old Main, Morrill Hall, and Margaret Hall.
Morrill Hall is at the center of this campus view. The Hub is to the left and was used as a post office, bookstore, and depot station for the Ames and College Railway.

A crowd is gathered along Morrill Road in front of Morrill Hall and Central Building (Beardshear Hall). A small tent appears between the two buildings. A period automobile is visible near the English Office Building in the background.
An aerial view of Beardshear Hall and the English Office Building, showing the top of the Marston Water Tower behind Beardshear Hall
State Gymnasium view from Union Drive, 1913

The State Gymnasium is viewed from the intersection of Welch Road and Union Drive, looking west, with the College Hospital on the right. A wagon with two horses is driving on the road that passes by a grove of trees where Friley Hall now stands. Surrounding trees and houses on the west side of campus are also visible.
The fire at the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn in 1901. The building was of frame construction with a gable roof. Flames are engulfing the roof as a number of onlookers watch.

A flock of Shropshire and Rambouillet sheep appear in this photograph along with the Hog Barn in the distance. The Farm Crops Laboratory can be seen in the background to the right side in the photograph.

In a wintertime scene, the Hog Barn is seen in the distance through the trees. According to an interoffice communication from William F. LaGrange this photograph shows the barn as located on the north side of fampus in the early 1920's, north of the Women's Gym and close to the railroad tracks. The road shown in the photograph came up to the Dairy Building and the Agricultural Annex.

The brick Horse Barn on the left, built around 1900, with the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn and the Judging Pavilion on the right. Two beef cattle are shown being led into the Pavilion as several men also approacch the area.
A twenty-mule team pulling a threshing machine. Six men are driving and working the machine while one walks alongside the mule team.

Several men on a float hitched to a pair of horses. Two other horses are hitched to a piece of farming equipment. The west-side of the original building and the 1903 north addition are in the background. The photograph has been stamped in ink "FILEPRINT NOT TO BE REMOVED FROM THIS FILE."
The front of Catt Hall with the greenhouse to the right. Trees in the foreground frame the scene.
The front of Catt Hall. The greenhouse and part of the Horticulture building can be clearly seen.
The side of Catt Hall and the greenhouse building beside it. The surrounding foliage obscures parts of both buildings.

The front of Catt Hall although part of the building is obscured by trees. An artillery cannon and railroad tracks are in the foreground. Several individuals in period dress are standing on the stairs and sidewalk in front of the building.
The side of Catt Hall with an open sliding door. Several individuals are standing in front of the door.
Carrie Chapman Catt Hall (Agricultural Hall) and greenhouses, 1919
The front and side of Catt Hall. Greenhouses can be seen in the foreground.
Carrie Chapman Catt Hall (Agricultural Hall), aerial view, 1913
An aerial view shows the front of Catt Hall and surrounding buildings.

The fire at the Iowa Agricultural College Experiment Barn in 1901. The building was of frame construction with a gable roof. Flames are engulfing the roof and traveling down the sides of the building as a crowd of onlookers watches.
Carrie Chapman Catt Hall (Agricultural Hall), aerial view, 1910
An aerial view of Catt Hall. The Farm Mechanics Lab, Old Horticulture Lab, greenhouse, Engineer's Cottage and a small portion of the old Cattle Barn are visible.
Photograph of the Sanitary Building, 1885-1912, which housed classrooms and laboratories for veterinary medicine
Photograph of the old Veterinary Hospital, 1985-1912
The Ames and College Railway (the Dinkey) car is stopped near Morrill Hall. A floral garden is planted along the tracks.
In this aerial view the Hub is to the left (west) of Morrill Hall.

View of the front (east) and north sides of Morrill Hall. Three woman, one with an umbrella, are walking northward and two men are walking southward across the front of the building. Old Main is on the left side of the photograph.
Period automobiles are parked in front of Morrill Hall and ivy is growing along the walls. The front of Central Building (Beardshear Hall) can be seen in the background.
Aerospace Laboratory, Foundry, Power Station, and Marston Water Tower, 1900-1908.
This group of buildings includes the Aerospace Laboratory, Foundry, and Power Station. The Marston Water Tower is in the background.
Morrill Hall is at the center of this campus view. The Hub is to the left and was used as a post office, bookstore, and depot station for the Ames and College Railway.
Morrill Hall and the Hub, October 16, 1929
Morrill Hall and the Hub, October 16, 1929.
Morrill Hall, Ladies Hall, Botany Hall
Campus buildings from left to right are: Morrill Hall, Ladies Hall (later known as Margaret Hall), and Botany Hall (later known as Carrie Chapman Catt Hall).
Looking northeast from Old Main, Ladies Hall (Later known as Margaret Hall) is on the left and Botany Hall (later known as Carrie Chapman Catt Hall) is on the right, 1896.
The north east corner of the Knoll seen through the trees. Part of the north and east sides of the buildings are also visible.
The southeast corner of the Knoll appears in the distance surrounded by trees and other shrubbery. A large expanse of lawn occupies the foreground.
A full view of the north side of the Knoll occupies the center frame. The wrap around porch, front entry way and the tower are clearly visible.
This northeast view of the Knoll features the porte cochere, ivy covered tower and a bit of the front entryway. A small part of the wrap around porch is also visible.
Centered on Marston Hall. Marston Water Tower is behind the tree on the left, as well as some of the Engineering shops.

Aerospace Engineering Annex, Marston Water Tower, Marston Hall. For cover of Iowa Engineer, November 22, 1913.
Marston Hall (the building on the right) in the 1920s.
The front of Catt Hall. In the foreground is a driver and a team of Percherons drawing a piece of lawn equipment.
Carrie Chapman Catt Hall (Agricultural Hall), aerial view, 1913
An aerial view shows the front of Catt Hall and surrounding buildings.

Several men on a float hitched to a pair of horses. Two other horses are hitched to a piece of farming equipment. The west-side of the original building and the 1903 north addition are in the background. The photograph has been stamped in ink "FILEPRINT NOT TO BE REMOVED FROM THIS FILE."
